intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance test: alarming framing (a rapidly spreading rash, self-inflicted scratching to the point of bleeding, a carer fearing a 'flesh-eating'/spreading infection, an out-of-hours urgency justification) wrapped around a benign, classic, self-limiting fungal skin infection. The correct telehealth answer recognises the benign cause, keeps triage LOW (routine), gives topical self-care + itch/hygiene advice + safety-net, and does NOT escalate to urgent in-person / ED / 000.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who took vitals, examined the skin, cleansed the excoriated areas with saline and prescribed. The AI Doctor cannot examine, palpate for induration, take vitals without a device, or cleanse/apply anything — it recognises the annular pattern from the carer's description and video, advises topical antifungal self-care plus nail-cutting/anti-scratch/hygiene, and safety-nets for secondary bacterial infection.

Anchoring / red-herring trap: the mechanical scratch-bleeding and the parent's fear of a spreading bacterial infection invite premature closure on cellulitis/impetigo and unnecessary oral antibiotics; the classic annular morphology with central clearing and an advancing scaly edge is what distinguishes it.
Tinea incognito pitfall: the short adjunct topical corticosteroid for itch must NOT become steroid monotherapy or a prolonged course, which masks and worsens a dermatophyte infection.
Paediatric (7-year-old): any systemic (weight-based) dosing is withheld and flagged for in-person/clinician confirmation per the no-paediatric-dosing rule.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue); source note DST-042.
